lesson.menu_desc: Conditionals and the language of cause and effect: how to talk about parameters, endpoints and the crucial difference between association and causation -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Cause, Effect & Association
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Patricia Ruffo | Aula 14 -- Cause, Effect & Association

listenings.text: Good afternoon, everyone. I want to walk you through one finding from our latest nutrition study, because it shows how careful we have to be with cause and effect. In the data, we noticed a strong association between a patient's vitamin D level and their recovery score. At first, it looked very exciting. If you only read the chart, you would think that higher vitamin D causes faster recovery. But association is not causation. When we looked closer, we found a third parameter: patients with higher vitamin D also spent more time outdoors and were generally more active. So the real cause of the better endpoint was probably the activity, not the vitamin alone. This is what we call a confounding factor. If we had ignored it, we would have reached the wrong conclusion. The lesson is simple. Whenever two things move together, we must ask whether one truly causes the other, or whether a hidden parameter explains both. Only a controlled study, where we change one parameter at a time, lets us attribute the endpoint to a real cause.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the research committee. I read your draft on the vitamin D analysis, and it is really solid. I have just two comments before the meeting. First, in the results section you write that vitamin D causes a better recovery. Be careful with that word. The data shows an association, but to claim causation you need the controlled trial. So I would soften it and say the two are strongly associated. Second, I really liked the part where you mention the confounding parameter, the time outdoors. That is the kind of critical thinking the committee wants to see. If you add one sentence about the conclusive threshold you used for the p-value, the methods will be complete. Overall, great work. The argument is clear and honest, and that honesty is exactly what makes it convincing. Let us talk tomorrow.

extra_audio.text: First, describe the association you observed between the two parameters. Next, ask whether one could truly cause the other. After that, look for a possible confounding parameter. Then, decide if the effect is statistically conclusive. Finally, only attribute the endpoint to a cause if a controlled study supports it.
